CHEMISTRY QUIZ — Acids, Bases and Salts
Class: SSS2 | Board: WAEC/NERDC | Time: 20 minutes

Q1. [EVALUATE] [Bloom: EVALUATE] [Marks: 4]
Aminu pours lemon juice and then baking soda into separate beakers of water. He measures a pH of 3 for lemon juice and 9 for baking soda solution.
Evaluate which substance is a stronger acid or base. Justify your answer using pH scale evidence and name TWO industries in Nigeria where each substance is used commercially.

Q2. [MULTIPLE_CHOICE] [Bloom: EVALUATE] [Marks: 2]
A student in Kano adds excess zinc to dilute hydrochloric acid. She argues the reaction will stop when the acid is used up. Assess whether her conclusion is correct:
A) Correct — zinc reacts with HCl until HCl is exhausted
B) Incorrect — the reaction stops when zinc is used up
C) Correct — zinc reacts with HCl until zinc is exhausted
D) Incorrect — the reaction never stops
Answer: A
Explanation: Zinc is in excess; therefore HCl (limiting reagent) is used up first.

Q3. [SHORT_ANSWER] [Bloom: EVALUATE] [Marks: 4]
Fatima argues that all salts are neutral. Critique this claim using named examples of acidic salts and basic salts found in everyday Nigerian households.

Q4. [EVALUATE] [Bloom: EVALUATE] [Marks: 4]
A factory in Port Harcourt discharges waste water with pH 2 into a local stream. Judge the environmental impact of this discharge and argue whether limestone (CaCO₃) would be an appropriate neutralising agent. Give your reasoning.

Q5. [SHORT_ANSWER] [Bloom: EVALUATE] [Marks: 3]
Assess the strengths and limitations of using litmus paper versus a pH meter for testing water quality in a Nigerian secondary school laboratory with limited resources.
reference: A WAEC/NERDC Chemistry quiz on Acids, Bases and Salts for SSS2 requiring evaluation and justification using Nigerian industrial and environmental contexts.
subject: chemistry
